metadata.title: Base code review rubric
metadata.description: Default severity rules for /gaia-code-review covering SOLID violations, complexity, naming, error handling, and code duplication. Layer 1 of the four-layer rubric composition (base + regimes + domain + project).

severity_rules.pattern: Class or module bears more than one responsibility — handles unrelated concerns or has multiple reasons to change.
severity_rules.description: Single Responsibility Principle violation. Splitting unrelated concerns improves cohesion and reduces blast radius of future changes.
severity_rules.remediation: Extract each responsibility into its own class or module with a focused public surface.

severity_rules.pattern: Adding new behaviour requires modifying existing code paths instead of extending via polymorphism, strategy, or composition.
severity_rules.description: Open-Closed Principle violation. Closed for modification, open for extension keeps existing behaviour stable while new variants land.
severity_rules.remediation: Introduce an abstraction (interface, strategy, or hook) so new variants extend rather than mutate existing logic.

severity_rules.pattern: Subclass overrides a parent method with a contract that breaks substitutability — narrows return type, throws unexpected exceptions, or changes invariants.
severity_rules.description: Liskov Substitution Principle violation. Callers that depend on the parent contract break unexpectedly when handed the subclass.
severity_rules.remediation: Either preserve the parent contract or favour composition over inheritance for divergent behaviour.

severity_rules.pattern: Interface forces implementers to depend on methods they do not use — fat or god-interfaces with unrelated capabilities.
severity_rules.description: Interface Segregation Principle violation. Smaller, role-specific interfaces reduce coupling and make mocks/stubs cheaper.
severity_rules.remediation: Split the interface along usage seams; keep each interface focused on one collaborator role.

severity_rules.pattern: High-level module imports a concrete low-level module directly instead of depending on an abstraction injected at construction time.
severity_rules.description: Dependency Inversion Principle violation. Hard-wiring concrete dependencies blocks substitution in tests and across environments.
severity_rules.remediation: Inject the dependency through a constructor or factory; depend on the abstraction.

severity_rules.pattern: Function or method has cyclomatic complexity > 10 (more than ten linearly independent paths).
severity_rules.description: Cyclomatic complexity above 10 correlates with defect density and review fatigue. Decomposition restores reviewability.
severity_rules.remediation: Extract guard clauses, replace nested conditionals with early returns, or split the function along its logical seams.

severity_rules.pattern: Function exceeds 50 executable lines or nests control flow more than three levels deep (cognitive complexity proxy).
severity_rules.description: Cognitive complexity above the project threshold makes the code hard to hold in working memory during review.
severity_rules.remediation: Decompose into named helpers, flatten conditionals, or replace deep nesting with early returns.

severity_rules.pattern: Identifier uses single letters, abbreviations, or domain-foreign words outside accepted conventions (loop counters and standard idioms exempted).
severity_rules.description: Names should reveal intent; opaque identifiers force readers to reverse-engineer purpose from usage.
severity_rules.remediation: Rename to a domain-meaningful term that describes the value or its role.

severity_rules.pattern: Function name describes mechanism rather than intent (e.g., `processData`, `handleStuff`) or violates the project's verb-first convention.
severity_rules.description: Function names that document mechanism not intent obscure what the call accomplishes.
severity_rules.remediation: Rename to an intent-revealing verb phrase (e.g., `calculateInvoiceTotal` instead of `processData`).

severity_rules.pattern: Class or constant violates the project's case convention (PascalCase for types, SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE for module-level constants in most stacks).
severity_rules.description: Inconsistent casing fragments the codebase and complicates linting and grep-based navigation.
severity_rules.remediation: Apply the project's casing convention; configure a linter rule so the convention is enforced going forward.

severity_rules.pattern: `catch` block is empty or only logs without rethrowing, recovering, or surfacing a structured error to the caller.
severity_rules.description: Swallowed exceptions hide failure modes and block upstream recovery. Silent failures are an availability risk.
severity_rules.remediation: Either handle the error (compensating action), wrap and rethrow with context, or propagate as a typed error result.

severity_rules.pattern: Function calls a fallible operation (network, filesystem, parser) without checking the result or wrapping in a try/catch — exceptions propagate unhandled.
severity_rules.description: Unhandled fallible operations crash the process or leak partial state to the caller.
severity_rules.remediation: Wrap the call site, define a recovery policy (retry, fail-fast, fallback), and surface a typed error to callers.

severity_rules.pattern: Generic `catch (Exception)` / `catch (Throwable)` / bare `except` masks programmer errors alongside expected failures.
severity_rules.description: Catching every exception treats logic bugs the same as expected failures and prevents the runtime from surfacing them.
severity_rules.remediation: Catch only the specific exception types the call site can recover from; let the rest propagate.

severity_rules.pattern: Two or more functions share more than 10 contiguous lines of nearly-identical logic differing only in literals or variable names.
severity_rules.description: Copy-paste duplication multiplies maintenance cost — fixes and new requirements have to be applied to every clone.
severity_rules.remediation: Extract the shared logic into a helper, parameterising the differing literals.

severity_rules.pattern: Magic literals (status codes, URL fragments, retry counts, timeouts) repeated across multiple call sites without a named constant.
severity_rules.description: Duplicated literals diverge silently as the code evolves and obscure their intent at the call site.
severity_rules.remediation: Hoist the literal to a named constant in a shared module; reference it from every call site.
